caption.text: The early 1940s to the mid-1960s saw one of many zeniths in American public housing projects, and Boston’s Jamaica Plain neighborhood was no different in this regard. Bromley-Heath, consisting of Bromley Park (pictured here) and Heath Street, is but one of these housing projects.

Walking around Heath and Center Streets today, one can still see the architectural lineage of these housing projects and how they changed the makeup of the neighborhood. But Bromley-Heath did not emerge from nothing…

Where did the residents of the area work? Burton Brewery can be seen right next to the main development here. Both German Protestants and Jews moved to the United States across the mid-19th Century. While many found homes in Pennsylvania and the Midwest, many found themselves arriving in Jamaica Plain and simply never left. [Source](https://www.jphs.org/victorian-era/bostons-lost-breweries.html#gsc.tab=0)

Burton Brewery was owned by one J.K. Souther. This small brewery bottled drinks such as Burton Ale, Bull’s Head and Special Porter. Americans had developed a taste for German-style lager drinks following the Civil War, and breweries such as these ably served demand. [Source](https://globalboston.bc.edu/index.php/home/immigrant-places/jamaica-plain/)

During prohibition, Burton moved to soft drinks such as Moxie. This was one of many breweries that pivoted or closed during this period. Much of the workforce for these small breweries would have been German Jews. [Source](https://www.jphs.org/locales/2005/10/15/bromley-heath-public-housing-development-history.html#gsc.tab=0)

Just across the street from Bromley-Heath is the former site of the Highland Spring Brewery. Founded by German immigrant Henry Rueter in 1867, this five-story building produced Irish-style Highland Springs Ale. A large refrigeration building was added in 1895. [Source](https://www.jphs.org/victorian-era/bostons-lost-breweries.html#gsc.tab=0)

How did so many breweries end up near Bromley-Heath? Roxbury’s merger with Boston in 1868 led to an abundance of cheap land in the area, and the plentiful groundwater in Stony Brook and Mission Hill meant Rueter had ample supply to produce ale. Before refrigeration, ice from Boston’s ponds served to keep the product cool. [Source](https://www.jphs.org/victorian-era/bostons-lost-breweries.html#gsc.tab=0)

The Highland building would serve many purposes over the years. Shut down after Prohibition in 1918, the plant would be used as storage by the Oliver Ditson music company for a period, and the Croft Company would re-open it as a brewery from 1934 to 1953. Today, the Highland building is an apartment complex. [Source](https://www.jphs.org/victorian-era/bostons-lost-breweries.html#gsc.tab=0)

Moving onto 1888, the John R. Alley Brewery serves as another example of the types of industry around the area, and the workers who comprised it. Named for its owner, one of Henry Rueter's business partner, this brewery was outfitted with state-of-the-art refrigeration infrastructure. [Source](https://historicboston.org/brews-and-mortar-the-future-of-eblana-brewery/)

Alley Brewery produced Eblana Irish Ale, distinct from the English porters and German lagers we’ve seen before. Nearly a quarter of Jamaica Plain’s population was Irish in the years leading up to Bromley-Heath’s construction, and many may have worked here.[Source](https://historicboston.org/brews-and-mortar-the-future-of-eblana-brewery/)

The breweries on and around Bromley-Heath tell us so much about the people who lived in Jamaica Plain at the time, from where they were from to where they worked. But breweries were not Jamaica Plain’s only industry… (Image: "The Making of the Perfect Martini", Guy Buffet)
Here we can see the Thomas Plant Shoe Factory. Compared to the breweries we’ve seen already, this factory is utterly massive, covering a large part of the modern Bromley Park housing complex.

Thomas Gustave Plant built his factory in 1900. Its principal products were Queen Quality Shoes, a brand of women’s shoes popular in the first few decades of the 20th Century. This state-of-the-art facility was the largest shoe manufacturer in the United States, and claimed to be the largest shoe manufacturer in the word. [Source](https://www.jphs.org/locales/2004/1/5/thomas-g-plant-shoe-factory-and-queen-quality-shoes.html#gsc.tab=0)

Much of the Plant Shoe Factory’s workforce was immigrants; Jamaica Plain’s population in 1880 was 25% Irish and 14% German. Many of these immigrants found themselves working at the breweries and factories of Jamaica Plain, while others worked in bars and shops supporting this population. [Source](https://globalboston.bc.edu/index.php/home/immigrant-places/jamaica-plain/)

Women in particular found ample opportunities at the Plant Shoe Company. The garment industry hired particularly high numbers of women at a time when few other industries would, and benefitted from a large labor union. Of the 5,000 people working in the factory in 1913, half of them were women. [Source](https://globalboston.bc.edu/index.php/home/immigrant-places/jamaica-plain/)
As we move to 1906, the Plant Shoe factory has gotten even larger. Plant wished to avoid running afoul of the powerful garment workers union, and beautified the surrounding area to appease them.

You can see echoes of this across the area today, from the Lucretia Crocker school just next door and Bromley Park surviving as a name to this day. [Source](https://www.jphs.org/locales/2004/1/5/thomas-g-plant-shoe-factory-and-queen-quality-shoes.html#gsc.tab=0)

The Thomas Plant Shoe Factory’s proximity to the railroad made it easy to receive unrefined leather and ship Queen Quality Shoes nationwide. Looking at the site of the modern Jackson Square MBTA station, you can see a building owned by R.A. and A.C. Lowell. These railway depots would have been vital for both processes.

A post-WW1 strike led to Plant selling the factory to United Shoe Company, who would run the business until shoemaking moved to other factories. For a time, the factory served as affordable housing, art studios, and small businesses. [Source](https://www.jphs.org/locales/2004/1/5/thomas-g-plant-shoe-factory-fire.html#gsc.tab=0)

The former Plant Shoe Factory served as a pillar of the local community, until it caught fire in April of 1976. Believed to be arson, the fires reached nearly 200 feet into the air and destroyed nearly all of the building’s wooden interior. Today, the old smokestack is all that remains, and the site of the factory is a supermarket and strip mall.[Source](https://www.jphs.org/locales/2004/1/5/thomas-g-plant-shoe-factory-fire.html#gsc.tab=0)

The Plant Shoe Factory survived for most of the 20th Century, and changed alongside Jamaica Plain. From demographics to technological and industry, life changed decade by decade for Jamaica Plain’s residents.

However much the area changed, however, the factory remained. Whether a shoe factory, art studios or housing, the factory was still the same building that had been erected nearly 80 years before, reinventing itself as Jamaica Plain reinvented itself.

We know who the people who lived in the area, and where they worked. But how did they live in the years before Bromley-Heath? What did housing look like beside these old breweries and factories? This map of 1930s Jamaica Plain, just before the earliest developments, can show us a lot.

The Great Depression had laid Jamaica Plain low. Home buying had slowed to a crawl by the early 1930s, and the federal government aimed to intervene. The Wagner-Stegall Act of 1937 provided government funds to local housing projects, and Boston aimed to use these funds to revitalize Heath Street. [Source](https://www.jphs.org/locales/2005/10/15/bromley-heath-public-housing-development-history.html#gsc.tab=0)

April of 1939 saw the area between Heath Street and Centre Street selected as a site for redevelopment, along with homes around them. Looking at the map would not show it, but many of the luxuries we take for granted were not features of these homes. 92 % of these homes lacked central heating, 47% lacked hot water and 20% even lacked bathrooms. [Source](https://www.jphs.org/locales/2005/10/15/bromley-heath-public-housing-development-history.html#gsc.tab=0)

The area was both deteriorating rapidly and close enough to public transport to make development worth it, in the eyes of authorities. Nearly 1,300 people lived in the area, and would have to move when construction began in 1940. [Source](https://www.jphs.org/locales/2005/10/15/bromley-heath-public-housing-development-history.html#gsc.tab=0)

The Heath Street buildings still stand today, and shape the visual character of the neighborhood. The brick buildings were designed to have simple facades and conform to a universal architectural standard.

Housing construction would slow after the war, but these new buildings would pay off sooner than anyone may have imagined. In a time when Boston’s naval facilities needed more workers than ever, Heath Street’s housing allowed Boston’s working population to expand to meet new demand.

The Bromley Park development was similarly massive. A large Ward Baking Company factory takes up much of the modern development, possibly the same Ward Company in New York that would later become Hostess.

Looking closer at the houses, you may notice that some of the names are not what you might expect. Italian immigration in the area surged in the years before Bromley was built, and many of these immigrants found themselves working at the Plant Shoe Factory alongside German and Irish-Americans.

This streetsign, at the intersection of Chestnut Ave and Centre Street, is named after one Peter R. Mutascio, an Italian-American captain who died during the Second World War. One Joseph Mutascio lived not too far from this intersection. [Source](https://www.jphs.org/locales/2005/10/15/bromley-heath-public-housing-development-history.html#gsc.tab=0)

Italian-Americans in Jamaica Plain gained much in the way of economic and political power leading up to Bromley Park’s construction. Not far from this development is the Jamaica Plain Italian American Social Club on Albert Street, who likely helped push for Mutascio streetsign. [Source](https://www.jphs.org/locales/2005/10/15/bromley-heath-public-housing-development-history.html#gsc.tab=0)

Much like that streetsign, Bromley Park was a post-war project. 1949’s Housing Act gave Boston the money needed to redevelop the area, largely consisting of tenement housing that hadn’t been meaningfully maintained for decades. [Source](https://www.jphs.org/locales/2005/10/15/bromley-heath-public-housing-development-history.html#gsc.tab=0)

Compared to Heath Street, these buildings were tall, designed to match the similarly large Plant Shoe Factory alongside it. Today, with no factory alongside it, these buildings stand out as the largest and densest developments in the neighborhood. [Source](https://www.jphs.org/locales/2005/10/15/bromley-heath-public-housing-development-history.html#gsc.tab=0)

Bromley-Heath stands at several crossroads. Built across two distinct periods of American policy, the development bridges different eras in immigration, Boston’s industry, and even architectural taste. Jamaica Plain is no stranger to the old shifting towards the new; even today, large affordable housing developments are still being made along Centre Street.
